bioShort: I study how the brain listens to speech, to music, to the world. As a Postdoctoral Research Scientist at Stanford, I combine neuroscience, signal processing, and machine learning to decode the neural pathways of hearing. When I'm not in the lab, I'm behind the console producing music and designing game audio.
bioFull:
I am a Postdoctoral Research Scientist in the Department of Psychiatry & Behavioral Sciences at Stanford Medicine, where I lead independent and collaborative research on the neural mechanisms of speech communication and social interaction. My work focuses on speaker-listener brain coupling and auditory temporal integration during naturalistic communication, using techniques such as EEG, fMRI, and computational modeling.

I received my Ph.D. in Biomedical Engineering from the University of Rochester, where I developed novel methods for deriving auditory brainstem responses to naturalistic speech and music, and investigated the neural encoding of musical acoustics and higher-order features across the human auditory system.

I have extensive experience in signal processing, machine learning, and experimental neuroscience, with industry experience at Meta Reality Labs evaluating auditory experiences on AR/VR wearable devices. I am passionate about applying my expertise in audio, hearing science, and data analysis to improve quality of life and well-being.

I am also a music producer focused on composing, game music and sound design, as well as music-visual art, where I explore the creative and expressive potential of sound and technology.
researchInterests: Auditory neuroscience, hearing science, neural encoding of natural sounds (speech and music), speech communication, auditory brainstem response (ABR), psychoacoustics, electroencephalography (EEG), fMRI, computational modeling, signal processing, machine learning
